title: os_crash
description: An operating system crash takes down multiple services on a production server.

timeline.title: System Becomes Unresponsive
timeline.description: A production server stops responding to all network requests. SSH sessions are dropped.

timeline.title: IPMI Console Checked
timeline.description: Out-of-band management console shows a kernel oops and system hang with a frozen process table.

timeline.title: Services Alert Firing
timeline.description: All monitoring checks for services on this host transition to CRITICAL. Users report outage.

timeline.title: Forced Reboot Required
timeline.description: Attempts to gracefully recover the OS fail. An emergency reboot via IPMI is issued.

timeline.title: System Reboots
timeline.description: Server reboots successfully. OS starts and filesystem check runs automatically.

timeline.title: Services Restored
timeline.description: All services restart cleanly. Crash dump is preserved for root cause analysis.

node-1.title: OS Hang Detected
node-1.situation: A production server is completely unresponsive. SSH connections time out and monitoring shows all services critical. The IPMI console shows a kernel oops message and a frozen state. The server hosts several critical microservices.
node-1.question: What is your immediate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Notify stakeholders of the outage, attempt to capture the crash state via IPMI console screenshot, and prepare to issue an emergency reboot via IPMI if recovery is not possible.

options.label: B
options.text: Immediately press the physical power button to hard-power-cycle the server without any investigation.

options.label: C
options.text: Wait 30 minutes for the OS to recover on its own before taking any action.

node-2.title: Crash Data Capture
node-2.situation: You have captured a screenshot of the kernel oops. The error references a third-party kernel module. The system is unrecoverable without a reboot. You need to trigger an emergency reboot while preserving crash diagnostic data.
node-2.question: How do you proceed?

options.label: A
options.text: Trigger a reboot via IPMI (using NMI to generate a crash dump if kdump is configured), then allow the system to reboot and collect the crash dump for post-incident analysis.

options.label: B
options.text: Issue a hard power-off immediately via IPMI without attempting to generate a crash dump.

node-3.title: Post-Reboot Validation
node-3.situation: The server has rebooted and the OS has started. A filesystem check ran and completed cleanly. Services are starting up. You need to confirm a full and clean recovery.
node-3.question: What do you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Verify all services have started successfully, run integration smoke tests, check system logs for any lingering errors, and confirm monitoring returns to green.

options.label: B
options.text: Assume services started correctly because the OS booted and close the incident immediately.

node-4.title: Root Cause Analysis
node-4.situation: Services are healthy. The crash dump analysis identifies a faulty third-party kernel module that was loaded after the last OS update. The module version is known to have a bug fixed in a newer release.
node-4.question: What is your next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Update the faulty kernel module to the fixed version, test in a staging environment, schedule the update for production during the next maintenance window, and document the incident.

options.label: B
options.text: Leave the faulty module in place and monitor to see if the crash recurs before patching.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: You successfully managed the OS crash by capturing diagnostic data, performing a controlled emergency reboot, validating service recovery, and identifying and scheduling remediation for the faulty kernel module.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
